title: RA.975 livwutang

translation.content: Where does sentimentality fit on the dance floor? For Liv Klutse, AKA livwutang, the answer is everywhere. The New York-based DJ is guided by a deep desire to stir connection, finding themes and points of correspondence across an impressively broad range of sounds, tempos and eras. This emotional intuition lends Klutse's sets a depth few others can match. Her selections can seem unpredictable, rooted in an appreciation for feeling over genre—hard and soft sounds are carefully balanced with surrealism and bursts of nostalgia (for instance, the Lazy Dog bootleg edit of Everything But The Girl's "Tracey In My Room"). But as versatile as she is, a few signature traits colour her style, such as a love for dubwise music and rhythms from across the Black diaspora, alongside a refreshingly introspective energy that invites dancers to find moments of meditation—even during peak time. A former staffer at our New York office, Klutse has long merited an RA Podcast. Her slow-burning blends and mystical selections have graced near-enough every major festival and club out there, from Dekmantel and Sustain-Release to Nowadays, where she's been a resident since 2022. Despite this cross-continental touring schedule, she still plays plenty of grassroots venues—testament to her days with TUF collective and Orphan Radio in Seattle, as well as her enduring belief in the power of DIY, community-orientated dance music. "How did you come down off life?" James Massiah asks at the beginning of RA.975. Across nearly 90 minutes, Klutse's downright beautiful mix seeks answers to this question, reflecting on hedonism in times of political decay. She's in full-on dub mode, moving through meditative bass weight, glitchy house and flat-out weirdo techno with a deftness we've come to expect from one of the most promising, singular voices in dance music right now. She's got there all on her own terms—and we couldn't be prouder. -Nyshka Chandran What have you been up to recently? I've had the last month "off" from touring and have spent the longest stretch of time at home in a while. Just been trying to recover from burnout and get reacquainted with taking care of myself, my creativity, catching up with the lives of people I care about and going out and playing in New York a bit. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? When I was working on the first draft of the mix (which I was originally meant to record in October), I was thinking about navigating a crumbling empire and reflecting on the hedonism I see (and sometimes participate in) when touring, playing or partying. Only one track (actually a poem) from that original tracklist ended up making the cut, which I used as a guidepost when selecting music to include: "Opiate" by James Massiah. I'd like to give an honorable mention to MHYSA's "before the world ends" and Bonaventure's "Both ft. Hannah Black," which I wanted to include in the mix, but couldn't. I've learned not to force tracks into a mix if my brain isn't arranging them in a way I'm creatively satisfied with. They will probably make future appearances though in other recordings : ) This iteration of the mix still carries that intention, but less explicitly so. You were part of the RA team until early 2024. Since shifting your focus to global touring, what have been the biggest challenges and most rewarding aspects of the transition? It's been a mixed experience that has brought me both closer to and further from people I love and my own creativity. On the one hand, I have more space and time during the week, but my relationships feel more distant because I'm constantly moving from place to place. On the other, I've had the opportunity to meet and grow closer to people I potentially would never have met and learn about and be influenced by scenes I'd only glimpsed from afar. Your motto is "play weirder, not harder." Where does this ethos come from, and where have you felt the most freedom to act on it? From the perspective of the DJ booth, it's easy to feel a compulsion to play things that are harder or with clear moments when I feel like the crowd isn't responding in the way I'd like. But so many times when I'm a dancer and I feel that tension from the other perspective, I'm not craving the urge to dance harder or faster: I want to hear something to facilitate sinking deeper into the basins of my mind or something where I can get creative with how I move in response. I try to remember that when I play. I've felt the most freedom to apply that ethos in the Pacific Northwest, New York, Waking Life… anywhere where I have the ability to stretch out with a contingent of patient dancers, really. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? The vast, unending labyrinth that Tangent Gallery becomes during No Way Back—clichés are clichés for a reason. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I resonated with the emphasis on interconnectedness of oppression and liberation of marginalised people globally that the homies Kiernan Laveaux and PGZ gave in response to this question in their interview. And to add on to their responses, I think it's important to study and apply strategies that suggest how we can each participate in ways to care for each other in our neighbourhoods, relationships and communities in ways that reverberate outwards. Dean Spade's writing on mutual aid and adrienne maree brown's book, Emergent Strategy—which she defines as "strategy for building complex patterns and systems of change through relatively small interactions" and as "an adaptive, relational way of being"—have been particularly influential and helpful to me on this tip. Looking ahead, what are you most excited about in 2025—whether creative, personal or in the wider scene? I'm excited to be inspired by scenes continuing to figure out how to invest in their local dance music ecosystems as the wider industry becomes increasingly fraught and the wealth disparity grows.
translation.blurb: The in-demand US DJ unfurls dubby dance floor poetry.
